speaker: interviewer
text: It's Bernice Krantz. Today's date is December the 13th, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Odette Ross in London, England and this is Tape number 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, we have arrived in Nice, where there were consuls and consulates. And the aim for my brother and myself was to get to Portugal and then to England, also hoping my uncle and aunt would be able to get to Portugal and perhaps get into England, although that was very dicey, as they were not either a child or a father. And the English-- there was a war on, and they only allowed children of refugees or parents of refugees. We applied for a Spanish visa because, as I said, they wouldn't allow me to cross the Pyrenees, because I was too young. And we went regularly to the Spanish consulate to see whether our visa, our transit visa, had arrived and we were always told, no, no, no. Eventually, after about four months, I arrived one morning with my brother and in the book of names, I saw the name Robinson. I said to him, well, that's us. That's Rubinstein. I wasn't lying. For one moment, I saw the R and I thought it was us. He stamped our passport and I'll never know whether he was [INAUDIBLE] or couldn't read, or whether he was sorry for us, or whether we'd nagged him long enough, but anyway that day we were given a transit visa to-- transit visa to Spain and Portugal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that under your own name, or was it under Robinson?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In our status-- on our-- on our name status paper. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what also helped us to get a Spanish visa was the fact that the Jews were advised, if they could afford it in Belgium, to buy entry visas to places like Puerto Rico, Costa Rica, Cuba, Mexico. And we actually purchased a visa-- or my uncle purchased a visa-- to Bolivia, a [INAUDIBLE] which is there. On that visa is added the picture of my brother, because bearing the same name as my uncle, he sort of became my uncle's son, which would have made it much easier.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he didn't have a picture of you in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All of my cousins-- presumably because we were girls and were too young and there was no need for it to be on it. The reason they wanted his photograph is really a sort of a proof that he was their son and therefore was also Belgian. Life in Nice was fairly pleasant for us children. We went to school, which was run by brilliant academics from Poland and Germany, and this took place in the basement of an old hotel. And, of course, the lectures we were given were fascinating, because the standard was [INAUDIBLE] university. We didn't take exams or anything, but at least we had our little brains sharpened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were Jewish people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the weather was pleasant. And there were, at the beginning of 1942, not too many raids. We stayed in a place called the Hotel Continental, which is a small hotel where we rented some rooms and a tiny kitchenette. But it was also occupied by the Italian delegation. And as we all know now, except for Mussolini, the Italians were really very good to the Jews and didn't like the system. And it was suggested to my uncle one day that we all went to Italy, because the Italians were much nicer to us than the French. We had all sorts of minor problems in Nice, not really minor, by which there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody blackmailing us into having to pay more and more for food, for ration tickets, until one day my uncle had the courage to go to the police and denounce this man. And fortunately, all went off well and we weren't bothered or blackmailed later. When we finally received this transit visa, which was in April 1942, my brother and I left Nice on our own, with my uncle and aunt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the hope of getting to Portugal within two days and getting our visa at the Belgian consulate and then getting to England. We went to Perpignan and from Perpignan we took the train. And we got to the Spanish frontier-- I mustn't forget the Spanish were great fascists. Franco really was not a very nice human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were stopped. Our visa wasn't valid, they said. Why? Because the stamp didn't say that the transit for 48 hours-- was for 48 hours only. We then tracked back by thumbing a lift back to Perpignan. And we got to the-- How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went by train to Nice, to the frontier, but then the authorities wouldn't let us through. We went back to Perpignan, where there was a consul. But it was late in the evening and the consulate was just about to close. And I must have been fairly controlled up to now, but I burst into tears. And my brother, in a very controlled fashion, sort of told me to pull myself together. But then for some mysterious reason, they opened the door and let us go upstairs to the consulate, at which point my brother nudged me and said, this is the time to cry. But I didn't. Anyway, we got the extra 48 hours only. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I suspect that even Fascists and Nazis, some of them were sorry for children. I'm not talking about the Gestapo and the SS, but like this man who gave us a 48-hour transit. We then went back, caught another train, crossed, spent two days on the train in Spain just to get off at stations and buy a bit of food, collected some lice on the way, and we eventually got to Portugal at the very beginning of May, 1942. When we arrived in Lisbon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to the Belgian consulate. And we very gingerly sort of said downstairs at the reception that we were Lucien and Odette Rubinstein and we believe there's a visa waiting for us. At that point, a very nice young man came downstairs and put his arms on me, says, ah, he said, the Rubinstein children. I've been waiting for them for months. And they made us very welcome and offered us food and found a hotel for us. My parents were very fortunate. They were allowed to send out 50 pounds, I think a month, or 50 pounds once or twice to Portugal, because they knew there might be a wait for us before we'd be able to get some sort of transport from Portugal. And we got to this lovely little hotel. We knew we were near the end of our ordeal. And we also--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt and uncle were still with you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you leave them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nice. When we left Nice--

speaker: interviewer
text: You left them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on our own. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And in Perpignan when we went to the consul, there's another little anecdote which I think is-- is quite important. When we were at Perpignan my brother and I on our own went and had a cup of coffee. And as we sat down, I took off my anorak, which was equivalent to-- a jacket equivalent to an anorak-- and hung it on a coat hanger. And my brother pulled it away and said, never leave your coat. And, of course, I realized at that point that I was carrying diamonds. We actually both were carrying diamonds. We had little money, but we knew that if we had diamonds, we could probably always get some money with it. And from then we were on our own. I was nearly 15. My brother was 17. But we were perfectly able to cope with everything. We were old, you know. We were old for our age and we coped very well. And I remember in Portugal, though we were in touch with the Belgian embassy and consulate all the time to see when there would be a flying bird that was able to take us to England, I used to walk up and down the main street buying presents for my parents. I bought my mother a pair of gloves. I can't remember what I bought for my dad, but that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was fairly safe in Portugal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Portugal was absolutely safe. Portugal was neutral and absolutely safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Portugal longer than we would have liked, about three weeks. Every time we were told that they thought there were two places on the seaplane, which landed in Bristol, what actually happened was that the-- the soldiers and the airmen who were caught somewhere in occupied countries would then make their escape through the Pyrenees, get to Portugal, and they would have priorities, you know, on the planes because they were going to come to England to fight for their country and for England. Anyway, eventually, at the beginning of May one day we-- we got onto the plane and we arrived in Bristol.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that journey like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bumpy and we landed on the sea. And I don't remember much about seaplanes, but my husband seems to know that they were very much the in thing. They were mainly army personnel of all sorts of nationality.

speaker: interviewer
text: And not very big.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We got to Bristol and there we were welcomed. Then we were interrogated by the Army, the Navy-- what is it?-- the Air Force, and the Intelligence. They were extremely knowledgeable and extremely kind to us. They knew everything about us, but they just wanted to make sure that we were my parents' children and we were refugees-- after all, my brother was 17-- and that we weren't spies. We were shown maps of the airport in Antwerp. And we were asked to tell them what we knew about it, whether we knew that it had been enlarged or not. Anyway, they were obviously quite satisfied that we were who we were after a long time. And the intelligent man turned around and said, you can go home to your parents now, but I'm afraid your brother has to come into an internment camp. And I think everything was just too much. I was overawed. And I said, I can't leave him. I haven't left him for two years and I don't speak the language. And at the time, it only seemed normal. But when I think there was a war on and this man said to me, don't worry. One of us will take you all the way to London, but I'm afraid your brother has to come for interrogations for a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point, I had them telephone my parents, who by then knew we were in Portugal and were expecting us, and seeing it's at Primrose 2410. And my mother said, yes. And they said, you can collect your daughter Odette at Paddington Station tonight at 10 o'clock. And the next thing I heard was-- because my English was not terribly good-- are you there, Mrs. Rubinstein? I think it was all just a bit too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my brother seemed in great control. And I wasn't all that worried, because they explained to us that an internment camp was not a concentration camp, but somehow he would be useful to Britain, be able to give them more information. Well, my parents arrived at the station. My father, who looked 20 years older than he did two years ago, seemed like a white ghost. We didn't say very much. We just hugged, didn't say very much. And went to the [INAUDIBLE] court and I went to bed. And during that night, my father came and touched me just to see whether I was really there or whether it was a dream. And that was on a Thursday. I'm not quite sure. I think it was around the 10th. My parents were very anxious to visit my brother. And I don't quite know how they made all the necessary inquiries, but we were told that on the Saturday we would be able to go to the internment camp, which wasn't very far from London, and we were to visit him. And, in fact, by the Monday he was released.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to your aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will tell you in a moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just wanted to add this, that my father-- my mother turned around and said, do you know what happened to my parents? And I said, yes, they were sent to a camp, but I think they were too old to work, and we haven't heard from them since. And my father touched my mother and said, the child is overawed. She doesn't know what she's talking about. And only when my brother confirmed it, that they were in deep shock. I think that things were just too horrific to really believe. The young cousin who helped us across to Paris and who eventually got to Switzerland, his parents were arrested in a raid on a train between Antwerp and Brussels. And the mother wrote a postcard from [NON-ENGLISH], which is where the train was stopped. And when he stopped hearing from them, he wrote a letter to Mr. and Mrs. [INAUDIBLE], Birkenau. He said, he had no-- I knew it was a work camp. And his parents were in their late 40s. He said even in Switzerland, they couldn't believe-- they knew, but they blocked it out. They just couldn't believe in 1943, even then, that they-- that it--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My uncle and aunt and the two daughters couldn't get a visa on the strength of the entry visa to Bolivia. But they finally purchased a transit visa through Spain and eventually got to Lisbon about three or four months after we did, that is, in October, 1942. Unfortunately, my uncle was over 40, so he was too old to volunteer to fight in England. Therefore, he didn't get an entry visa. But the Belgian Congo, which belonged to Belgium, allowed him in, so they spent the war years in the Belgian Congo. So both my uncle and ourselves were saved. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Another little anecdote I'd like to bring-- to talk about is we, my brother and I, had some diamonds in our purse. I don't know how much. I have no idea. And wherever we were, they said, anything to declare, well, they used to frisk. We said, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the same thing happened when we landed in Bristol. They said, do you have anything? We said no. And after a few days, or when we arrived, we took the little parcel and gave it to my father and it was, I suppose, worth quite a bit. And my father suddenly had a problem with the income tax people. You know, suddenly he's got sort of extra money. So he thought, well, the only thing you could do is go and tell them the truth. So he went and he said, my children just arrived and suddenly I've got a little capital, X, Y, Z. And they were extremely nice about it and accepted what he said. But it was very strange, because suddenly he would have, you know, had-- he then had enough money to start to work on his own. And they might have said, well, where did you get this capital?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the beauty was that how honest and trustworthy the British were. I suppose they knew who to trust and not to trust. And-- we have only come across kindness from the British. I know that they should have bombed the lines to the concentration camps in 1943. And there were foreign ministers who said, why should we fight a war to save the children of our enemies, et cetera, et cetera. But I must say that I never came across anything like that at all. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] My mother had already made inquiries about schools. I was 15 and my brother was 17. My mother, quite rightly, although I was horrified, threw me into Queens College, where I cried myself to sleep for three months, but I learned English. The staff were wonderful to me. The headmistress helped me with English and the [INAUDIBLE] who herself was-- came out of Germany, was fascinated by my story. I went to school for Queens College, which seemed to have a lot of daughters of diplomatic service. There were Swedes and Greeks, et cetera, et cetera. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was international, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strangely enough, I passed my matriculation very well after 15 months and went on to take a high school certificate. My brother went to [INAUDIBLE] College. He had always been an outstanding student. And as a child, he wanted to do philosophy. But somehow when he came to England, he felt he wanted to do something more pragmatic and he decided to do medicine. Although we spent two years together, we really very seldom discussed what happened. He was a very introvert child, [SIGHS] basically because he was born, I think, with a birthmark. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And really there was nothing to discuss. We had shared the experience. It's only later on that I would have liked to have talked to him. But he eventually emigrated to America and I didn't see-- I didn't see that much of him until just before he died, when we really started to communicate. I had a recurring dream for years of walking across the channel and never quite making it. In fact, I had this dream for about 20 years or so. When I went to school in England, I met, strange enough, a-- a young English girl who had been born in Belgium and was at school with me in 1934 and I recognized her. I became very friendly with her. She had a very jovial father. My father was a very serious, sad person, I think, because of the circumstances. He took the separation in a much worse fashion than my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I used to go and play with my girlfriend. This man used to say, don't you ever laugh? And my answer to that was, what was there to laugh about? I'm very grateful to that man, because he did teach me to laugh again. And of course, time, you know, helps to sort of heal the wounds. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think not just us, I think everybody who went through a terrible ordeal tried to block out the memories for a long time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And now there are very few people that I can sort of get, you know, more details from. Anyway, my brother and I both went to university. He became a doctor and a worldwide authority. In fact, they called him the greatest living authority in neuropathology. Ended up with a chair at Stanford and at the University of Virginia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at university?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Cambridge through the back door. By that I mean I was at London University. But we were evacuated in Cambridge. I had a wonderful couple of years there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: I studied English and French, majoring in French, because otherwise the study of English would have been too difficult. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I never quite understood how my parents allowed me to go to Cambridge until I realized that although I had been quite brave in Belgium, when the Germans started with the-- the Jews, I became terribly, terribly nervous and somehow to get out of London. And I think that's when my parents agreed for me to go away to university.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although they stayed in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stayed in London. It was a good thing, because my relationship with my mother was very difficult. I think that she thought she had found a child of 13, and I was a young adult of 15 with the mentality of a person of about 40. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met my husband in '46. I got married very young. I got married--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got terribly nervous about the bombing, and so my parents decided to go to Bournemouth and take me away. And we went to the Green Park, which I think had just opened. And there we came across a family whose son was in the army. And the adults decided that I should meet their son. And Arnold happened to be the son's best friend. And whilst he was asked to look after me while Stuart was in Cardiff, I started going out with him. And that's, you know, how it all happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you got married? You said you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not quite 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still at university or you hadn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I actually gave up, which was a mistake, but I mean, it was my own decision. Arnold had just come back from five years in India [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- he's South African, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which army was he-- who was he with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British Army. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he was in the British--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was very young when he came. And he was six years older than myself, and he was going to take me to quite a different world. And it's a long time ago. Either I could have waited and finished my-- my degree. One didn't-- in those days, young women didn't really go on studying after they were married. It was a completely different generation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have-- We have a son and a daughter. Our son is--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were both born in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. When?

speaker: interviewee
text: When. Oh. Doug was born in '48 and my daughter in '52, January of '52. They both have two sons. My son is a professor of theoretical physics at the University of Southampton. My daughter is a very successful headhunter. And the children are great. As we speak, my grandson-- my son's son-- is being interviewed at Oxford. And he's probably going to follow in his father's footsteps. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] When I went to my brother's funeral in Virginia--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years ago, in 1990. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were quite a lot of very southern-- southern American WASPish professors there from Georgia and Charlottesville, et cetera. And my sister-in-law wanted me to speak about the war years the evening of his funeral.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow I did this and came out with the point-- the salient points. And one of these professors turned around and said, can you imagine how many more brains, Jewish brains like Lucien's, were lost through the Holocaust? And it's the first time that I had really thought about it, but it's absolutely true. I suppose, you know, a great deal of great brains were lost. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how other people feel and react, and how much they're conditioned by their upbringing or whatever. But I know that with me, memories still come quite often, frequently and uninvited, and that my attitude to life and in life is conditioned by my childhood and teen year-- teen year experiences.
